title: Carbohydrate Calculator for Exercise | Hacé Cuentas
h1: How Many Carbohydrates Do You Need During Exercise?
description: Calculate carbohydrate needs by duration and intensity. Get personalized recommendations based on ACSM guidelines for runners, cyclists, and endurance athletes. Free calculator.
intro: This calculator estimates how many grams of carbohydrates you need to consume **during** aerobic exercise, based on your session duration, intensity level, and body weight. Unlike pre-workout fueling, intra-workout carbohydrate intake directly prevents glycogen depletion, maintains blood glucose, and delays fatigue. The core formula is derived from exercise physiology research: for sessions under 60 minutes, exogenous carbohydrates are generally unnecessary; for 60–90 minutes, 30–60 g/hr is recommended; for efforts over 90 minutes, up to 90 g/hr using a glucose+fructose blend can be fully oxidized by the body. Body weight influences total glycogen stores (~15 g/kg muscle mass), making it a key input for personalized recommendations.
keyTakeaway: **Carbs/hr = 30–90 g depending on intensity and duration; max oxidation rate is ~1 g/min for glucose alone, or ~1.5 g/min (90 g/hr) with a 2:1 glucose-to-fructose ratio** — exceeding these limits causes GI distress, not extra energy.
useCases: A marathon runner planning gel intake every 20–30 minutes for a 3–4 hour race to avoid hitting 'the wall' around mile 18–20.
A cyclist doing a 2-hour group ride at 70–75% VO₂max calculating whether one sports drink or two energy gels per hour better covers their 60 g/hr target.
A soccer or basketball player estimating halftime and in-game snack needs across a 90-minute match at variable intensities.
A triathlete completing an Ironman (8–12 hours) building a precise nutrition plan that maximizes the 90 g/hr carbohydrate oxidation ceiling without causing stomach upset.
A recreational gym-goer doing a 45-minute HIIT session checking whether mid-workout carbohydrates are actually needed or just add unnecessary calories.

faq.q: Do I need carbohydrates during exercise if my session is under 60 minutes?
faq.a: For most people, no. Muscle glycogen stores hold approximately 300–500 g of glycogen (~1,200–2,000 kcal), which is more than sufficient for sessions under 60 minutes. Research consensus from the ACSM indicates that exogenous carbohydrate intake during sub-60-minute efforts does not meaningfully improve performance for trained individuals. The exception is athletes who trained in a fasted state or those with medical conditions affecting glucose regulation.

faq.q: Why is the maximum carbohydrate absorption rate capped at 90 g per hour?
faq.a: The small intestine absorbs glucose primarily via the SGLT1 transporter, which saturates at approximately 60 g/hr. Fructose uses a separate transporter (GLUT5) with its own ~30 g/hr capacity. When these two transporters are used simultaneously — achieved by consuming a 2:1 glucose-to-fructose blend — the combined absorption ceiling reaches ~90 g/hr. Consuming more than 90 g/hr causes unabsorbed carbohydrates to remain in the gut, drawing in water osmotically and causing bloating, cramping, and diarrhea.

faq.q: What is the best carbohydrate-to-fructose ratio for endurance events over 2 hours?
faq.a: The evidence-backed ratio is **2:1 glucose to fructose**, which maximizes total carbohydrate oxidation rates up to 90 g/hr. A 1:0.8 ratio (e.g., maltodextrin:fructose) has also shown strong results in cycling research. Many commercial gels (e.g., GU Roctane, Maurten) already use these ratios. Avoid pure glucose or maltodextrin products when targeting intakes above 60 g/hr for sessions exceeding 2 hours.

faq.q: How does intensity level affect how many carbohydrates I need during a workout?
faq.a: Intensity determines how quickly you burn through glycogen. At low intensity (<55% VO₂max), fat oxidation dominates and glycogen is spared, reducing CHO needs. At moderate intensity (60–74% VO₂max), glycogen use accelerates — recommending 30–60 g/hr for sessions over 75 minutes. At high intensity (≥75% VO₂max), you rely almost exclusively on carbohydrates as fuel, requiring 60–90 g/hr for sessions exceeding 90 minutes to prevent the performance decline known as 'hitting the wall' (glycogen depletion).

faq.q: How does body weight factor into carbohydrate needs during exercise?
faq.a: Body weight correlates with total muscle mass, which determines glycogen storage capacity. Muscle glycogen stores average about 15 g per kg of muscle mass. A 90 kg athlete with greater muscle mass can store more glycogen (~450–550 g in muscles + ~90–110 g in liver) than a 60 kg athlete, meaning the larger athlete has a longer time-to-depletion at equivalent intensities. However, the per-hour intake recommendations (30–90 g/hr) are primarily driven by intestinal absorption limits, which are relatively consistent across body weights, not by body weight alone.

faq.q: Is a banana as effective as an energy gel for mid-exercise fueling?
faq.a: A medium banana (~118 g) provides approximately 27 g of carbohydrates with a roughly 50:50 glucose-to-fructose ratio, comparable to a standard energy gel (~25 g CHO). Bananas also provide potassium (~422 mg) and are easier on sensitive stomachs for some athletes. The trade-off is practicality: bananas are bulkier, harder to carry, and slower to eat while running or cycling at pace. For efforts under 2 hours, bananas are a perfectly valid gel substitute. For high-intensity racing or ultra-endurance, gels and liquid carbohydrates are generally more practical.

faq.q: What happens if I consume too many carbohydrates during exercise?
faq.a: Overconsumption beyond the 90 g/hr absorption ceiling leads to gastrointestinal distress: nausea, bloating, diarrhea, and stomach cramps — collectively called 'exercise-induced GI distress.' This is one of the most common causes of race-day DNFs (Did Not Finish) in endurance sports. Additionally, consuming more carbohydrates than needed in sub-60-minute sessions simply adds unnecessary caloric intake. The gut is trainable over 4–8 weeks, so athletes new to high CHO intake should gradually build up their per-hour consumption during training, not debut high rates on race day.

faq.q: Should I use sports drinks, gels, or solid food during exercise, and does it matter?
faq.a: The format matters primarily based on intensity and duration. Sports drinks (isotonic, ~6–8% CHO concentration) are absorbed quickly and also hydrate simultaneously — ideal for moderate intensities. Gels are calorie-dense and portable, preferred for high-intensity efforts where gastric emptying is slower. Solid foods (bananas, dates, chews) work well at lower intensities or early in long events, but should be phased out as intensity rises late in a race. The golden rule: whatever format you choose, **always practice it in training first** to assess gut tolerance before race day.

sources.name: NIH National Library of Medicine — Exogenous Carbohydrate Oxidation and Performance

NIH National Library of Medicine — Carbohydrate Intake and Exercise Performance: A Review

explanation:
## How It Is Calculated

Carbohydrate needs during exercise are calculated based on **duration** and **intensity**, with body weight used to contextualize glycogen stores and confirm baseline targets. The scientific consensus from the American College of Sports Medicine (ACSM) and peer-reviewed nutrition literature yields the following decision framework:

```
IF duration < 60 min:
  Carbs/hr = 0 g  (no exogenous CHO needed for most people)

IF duration 60–75 min:
  Carbs/hr = 30 g  (mouth-rinsing or small amounts sufficient)

IF duration 75–120 min AND intensity = moderate (60–74% VO₂max):
  Carbs/hr = 45 g

IF duration 75–120 min AND intensity = high (≥75% VO₂max):
  Carbs/hr = 60 g

IF duration > 120 min AND intensity = moderate:
  Carbs/hr = 60 g

IF duration > 120 min AND intensity = high:
  Carbs/hr = 90 g  (requires 2:1 glucose:fructose ratio for full absorption)

Total Session (g) = (Carbs/hr) × (duration_minutes / 60)
Gels equivalent   = Total Session (g) ÷ 25 g per gel
Bananas equivalent = Total Session (g) ÷ 27 g usable CHO per medium banana
```

The **90 g/hr ceiling** exists because the intestinal sodium-glucose transporter 1 (SGLT1) saturates at ~60 g/hr for glucose alone. Adding fructose, which uses the GLUT5 transporter, allows an additional 30 g/hr to be absorbed, totaling 90 g/hr — a finding confirmed in multiple cycling studies measuring exogenous carbohydrate oxidation rates.

---

## Reference Table

| Duration | Intensity | Recommended CHO/hr | Common Sources |
|---|---|---|---|
| < 60 min | Any | 0 g | Water only |
| 60–75 min | Low–Moderate | 30 g | ½ banana, 1 gel, 500 mL sports drink |
| 75–120 min | Moderate (60–74% VO₂max) | 45 g | 1 gel + 250 mL sports drink |
| 75–120 min | High (≥75% VO₂max) | 60 g | 2 gels or 750 mL sports drink |
| > 120 min | Moderate | 60 g | 2 gels or 1 banana + drink |
| > 120 min | High | 90 g | 3 gels (glucose+fructose) or mixed sources |

**Common food carbohydrate content at a glance:**

| Food/Product | Serving | CHO (g) | Glucose:Fructose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy gel (standard) | 1 packet (~40 mL) | 25 g | Varies (read label) |
| Medium banana (118 g) | 1 fruit | ~27 g | ~50:50 |
| Sports drink (Gatorade) | 500 mL | ~30 g | ~50:50 |
| Medjool date | 1 date (24 g) | ~18 g | ~45:55 |
| White bread | 1 slice (30 g) | ~15 g | ~100% glucose |
| Rice cake (plain) | 1 cake (9 g) | ~7 g | Mostly glucose |

---

## Typical Cases

**Case 1 — Recreational half-marathon runner (75 kg, 2 hours, high intensity):**
Duration > 120 min + high intensity → 90 g/hr target.
Total = 90 g/hr × 2 hr = **180 g CHO**.
Gel equivalent = 180 ÷ 25 = **7.2 gels** (or 4 gels + 2 bananas + 500 mL sports drink).
Timing: 1 gel every ~15–17 minutes starting at the 30-minute mark.

**Case 2 — Cyclist, 90-minute moderate ride (70 kg, 70% VO₂max):**
Duration 75–120 min + moderate intensity → 45 g/hr.
Total = 45 × 1.5 = **67.5 g CHO**.
Gel equivalent = 67.5 ÷ 25 = ~**2.7 gels** (practical plan: 2 gels + 500 mL sports drink).
Alternatively: 1 large banana + 1 gel covers ~52 g — close to target.

**Case 3 — HIIT gym session (80 kg, 50 minutes, high intensity):**
Duration < 60 min → **0 g exogenous CHO needed**.
Pre-workout glycogen stores (~1,200–1,600 kcal stored in muscle + liver) are more than sufficient for a sub-60-minute session. Consuming carbs mid-workout here adds ~100–200 unnecessary calories with no performance benefit for most people.

---

## Common Mistakes

1. **Eating carbs during sessions under 45 minutes**: Unless you trained fasted or are diabetic, glycogen stores fully cover short sessions. A 25 g gel adds ~100 kcal you don't need.
2. **Using only glucose-based gels for efforts over 90 g/hr**: SGLT1 saturates at ~60 g/hr for pure glucose. Using glucose-only products above 60 g/hr causes bloating, cramping, and diarrhea because unabsorbed sugars draw water into the intestine.
3. **Ignoring the warm-up window**: Most sports nutrition guidelines recommend starting carbohydrate intake at 30–45 minutes into the session, not at the start, to align with glycogen depletion timing.
4. **Counting total carbs from sports drinks without reading the label**: A 500 mL bottle of a hypotonic drink may only have 15–20 g CHO, while an isotonic drink has 30–35 g. Assuming all drinks are equivalent leads to underfueling.
5. **Relying on solid food late in a race**: Gastric emptying slows significantly during high-intensity exercise (>75% VO₂max). Solid foods that are fine at mile 5 of a marathon can cause nausea at mile 22. Liquids and gels are preferred as intensity increases.
6. **Not training the gut**: The gut's ability to absorb 90 g/hr is trainable. Athletes who suddenly attempt 90 g/hr on race day without practice in training frequently experience GI distress. Carbohydrate gut training takes 4–8 weeks of consistent practice.

---
